notes.title: Relations and Functions (Advanced) — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Types of relations/functions, composition, invertibility and binary operations.

## 1. Relations
- Reflexive, symmetric, transitive, **equivalence relations** (partition a set into equivalence classes).

## 2. Functions
- One-one (injective), onto (surjective), bijective.
- A function is invertible iff it is bijective; (f⁻¹)⁻¹ = f.

## 3. Composition
- (gof)(x) = g(f(x)); associative but not commutative.
- (gof)⁻¹ = f⁻¹og⁻¹.

## 4. Binary Operations
- Closure, associativity, commutativity, identity element, inverse element.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Invertibility requires bijection (both one-one AND onto).
- Composition is not commutative (fog ≠ gof in general).
- Identity for an operation must work for every element.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Equivalence relation → classes.
- Bijective ⇒ invertible.
- (gof)⁻¹ = f⁻¹og⁻¹.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
To prove invertibility, show one-one and onto, then construct the inverse explicitly.

notes.title: Inverse Trigonometric Functions — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Definitions, principal value branches and identities of inverse trig functions.

## 1. Principal Value Ranges
- sin⁻¹x ∈ [−π/2, π/2]; cos⁻¹x ∈ [0, π]; tan⁻¹x ∈ (−π/2, π/2).

## 2. Key Identities
- sin⁻¹x + cos⁻¹x = π/2; tan⁻¹x + cot⁻¹x = π/2; sec⁻¹x + csc⁻¹x = π/2.
- tan⁻¹x + tan⁻¹y = tan⁻¹((x+y)/(1−xy)) (with conditions on xy).
- 2tan⁻¹x = tan⁻¹(2x/(1−x²)) = sin⁻¹(2x/(1+x²)).

## 3. Conversions
- Convert between inverse functions using right-triangle representations.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Always respect the PRINCIPAL value branch (range restrictions).
- The addition formula for tan⁻¹ needs conditions on xy (add/subtract π).
- sin⁻¹(sinx) = x only within the principal range.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Principal ranges.
- Complementary identities (sum = π/2).
- 2tan⁻¹x conversions.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Watch the domain conditions in tan⁻¹ addition — a classic JEE trap with ±π adjustments.

notes.title: Matrices and Determinants — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Matrix algebra, determinants, inverse and solving linear systems.

## 1. Matrices
- Types: square, diagonal, identity, symmetric (Aᵀ=A), skew-symmetric (Aᵀ=−A).
- Matrix multiplication is associative, NOT commutative. (AB)ᵀ = BᵀAᵀ.

## 2. Determinants
- Properties: row/column swap changes sign; common factor; equal rows ⇒ det = 0.
- |AB| = |A||B|; |kA| = kⁿ|A| (n×n).

## 3. Inverse of a Matrix
- A⁻¹ = adj(A)/|A|, exists iff |A| ≠ 0 (non-singular).
- (AB)⁻¹ = B⁻¹A⁻¹.

## 4. Solving Linear Equations
- **Cramer's rule:** x = Δ₁/Δ etc. (Δ ≠ 0 → unique solution).
- Matrix method: X = A⁻¹B.
- Δ = 0: no solution or infinitely many (consistent/inconsistent).

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Matrix multiplication is not commutative.
- Inverse exists only if determinant ≠ 0.
- (AB)ᵀ = BᵀAᵀ and (AB)⁻¹ = B⁻¹A⁻¹ (order reverses).

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Symmetric/skew-symmetric; (AB)ᵀ=BᵀAᵀ.
- A⁻¹ = adj A/|A|.
- Cramer's rule & consistency.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
For system of equations, check Δ first: Δ≠0 unique; Δ=0 then examine Δ₁,Δ₂,Δ₃ for consistency.

notes.title: Continuity and Differentiability — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Continuity, differentiability and advanced differentiation techniques.

## 1. Continuity
- f continuous at a: LHL = RHL = f(a).
- Sum/product/quotient of continuous functions are continuous.

## 2. Differentiability
- Differentiable ⇒ continuous (converse not true, e.g. |x| at 0).

## 3. Differentiation Techniques
- Chain rule, product, quotient rules.
- Implicit differentiation; logarithmic differentiation (for y = f(x)^{g(x)}).
- Parametric: dy/dx = (dy/dt)/(dx/dt). Second derivative.

## 4. Theorems
- **Rolle's theorem** and **Lagrange's Mean Value Theorem** (MVT).

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Differentiability implies continuity, but not the reverse.
- Use logarithmic differentiation for variable base AND power.
- Check conditions before applying Rolle/MVT.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Continuity: LHL=RHL=f(a).
- Implicit, logarithmic, parametric differentiation.
- Rolle's & MVT.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
For functions like xˣ, take log first, then differentiate — standard JEE technique.

notes.title: Application of Derivatives — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Rate of change, tangents/normals, monotonicity and maxima/minima.

## 1. Rate of Change & Tangents
- dy/dx = slope of tangent. Normal slope = −1/(dy/dx).
- Related rates problems.

## 2. Monotonicity
- f'(x) > 0 → increasing; f'(x) < 0 → decreasing on an interval.

## 3. Maxima & Minima
- Critical points: f'(x) = 0. **First derivative test** (sign change) or **second derivative test** (f''>0 minima, f''<0 maxima).
- Absolute extrema on [a,b]: compare critical points and endpoints.

## 4. Approximations
- Δy ≈ (dy/dx)Δx for small changes.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- f'(x)=0 gives critical points, not necessarily extrema (check the test).
- Don't forget endpoints when finding absolute maxima/minima.
- Second derivative test fails when f''=0 (use first derivative test).

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Tangent/normal slopes.
- Increasing/decreasing via f'.
- Maxima/minima tests.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
For optimisation word problems, express the quantity in one variable, then set the derivative to zero.

notes.title: Integrals — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Indefinite & definite integration techniques and properties.

## 1. Indefinite Integrals
- ∫xⁿdx = xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C (n≠−1); ∫dx/x = ln|x| + C; ∫eˣdx = eˣ + C.

## 2. Methods
- **Substitution**, **integration by parts** (∫u dv = uv − ∫v du; ILATE order), **partial fractions**, trigonometric substitutions.

## 3. Definite Integrals
- ∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx = F(b) − F(a). Properties: ∫ₐᵇ f = ∫ₐᵇ f(a+b−x); ∫₀ᵃ f(x) = ∫₀ᵃ f(a−x).
- Even/odd: ∫₋ₐᵃ f = 2∫₀ᵃ f (even), 0 (odd).

## 4. Definite Integral as Limit of Sum
- Riemann sum definition.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Choose 'u' by ILATE in integration by parts.
- Use symmetry/king property to simplify definite integrals.
- Don't forget the constant C in indefinite integrals.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Substitution, by parts (ILATE), partial fractions.
- King property: ∫ₐᵇf(x)=∫ₐᵇf(a+b−x).
- Even/odd function integrals.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
The 'king property' (a+b−x) cracks many JEE definite-integral problems in one line.

notes.title: Application of Integrals — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Area under curves and between curves using integration.

## 1. Area Under a Curve
- Area between y = f(x), x-axis, x=a to x=b: ∫ₐᵇ |f(x)| dx.

## 2. Area Between Two Curves
- ∫ₐᵇ [f(x) − g(x)] dx where f(x) ≥ g(x) on [a,b].

## 3. With Respect to y
- Sometimes integrating w.r.t. y is easier: ∫ x dy.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Take the modulus where the curve dips below the x-axis (split the integral).
- Identify which curve is upper/lower before subtracting.
- Find intersection points to set limits.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Area = ∫|f(x)|dx.
- Between curves: ∫(upper − lower).
- Split at sign changes / intersections.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Always sketch the region and find intersection points first — prevents sign and limit errors.

notes.title: Differential Equations — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Order, degree and methods of solving differential equations.

## 1. Basics
- **Order** = highest derivative; **degree** = power of highest derivative (after removing radicals).

## 2. Methods
- **Variable separable:** f(y)dy = g(x)dx.
- **Homogeneous:** put y = vx.
- **Linear DE:** dy/dx + Py = Q; integrating factor IF = e^{∫P dx}; solution y·IF = ∫Q·IF dx.

## 3. Formation
- Form a DE by eliminating arbitrary constants (number of constants = order).

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Degree is defined only when the equation is a polynomial in derivatives.
- For linear DE, multiply through by the integrating factor.
- Don't forget the constant of integration.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Order & degree.
- Separable, homogeneous, linear (IF method).
- Eliminate constants to form DE.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Identify the DE type first (separable / homogeneous / linear) — that picks the method instantly.

notes.title: Vector Algebra — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Vectors, dot and cross products and their applications.

## 1. Basics
- |a| = √(a₁²+a₂²+a₃²). Unit vector â = a/|a|.
- Position vectors; section formula.

## 2. Dot Product (scalar)
- a·b = |a||b|cosθ = a₁b₁+a₂b₂+a₃b₃. Perpendicular ⇒ a·b = 0.
- Projection of a on b = (a·b)/|b|.

## 3. Cross Product (vector)
- a×b = |a||b|sinθ n̂; |a×b| = area of parallelogram. Parallel ⇒ a×b = 0.

## 4. Triple Products
- Scalar triple product [a b c] = a·(b×c) = volume of parallelepiped; = 0 if coplanar.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Dot product is scalar; cross product is a vector.
- a·b = 0 ⇒ perpendicular; a×b = 0 ⇒ parallel.
- Scalar triple product = 0 means vectors are coplanar.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Dot (cosθ), cross (sinθ).
- Projection formula.
- [a b c] = volume; 0 ⇒ coplanar.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Use the scalar triple product to test coplanarity and compute volumes in one step.

notes.title: Three Dimensional Geometry — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Lines and planes in 3D space.

## 1. Direction Cosines & Ratios
- l² + m² + n² = 1. Direction ratios proportional to direction cosines.

## 2. Line
- Vector form: r = a + λb. Cartesian: (x−x₁)/a = (y−y₁)/b = (z−z₁)/c.
- Angle between lines via direction ratios: cosθ = |b₁·b₂|/(|b₁||b₂|).

## 3. Plane
- Normal form: r·n̂ = d. Cartesian: ax+by+cz+d = 0 (normal (a,b,c)).
- Distance of a point from a plane: |ax₁+by₁+cz₁+d|/√(a²+b²+c²).

## 4. Line & Plane Relations
- Angle between line and plane; condition for line lying in a plane.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- For a line and plane, sinθ uses the normal (not cosθ).
- Coefficients (a,b,c) of plane give the normal vector.
- Use direction ratios consistently.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- l²+m²+n²=1.
- Line & plane equations.
- Point-to-plane distance.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Remember: angle between line and plane uses the normal (sinθ), between two planes uses the normals (cosθ).

notes.title: Probability (Bayes & Distributions) — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Conditional probability, Bayes' theorem and probability distributions.

## 1. Conditional Probability
- P(A|B) = P(A∩B)/P(B). Multiplication: P(A∩B) = P(A)P(B|A).
- Independent events: P(A∩B) = P(A)P(B).

## 2. Total Probability & Bayes
- Total probability: P(A) = Σ P(Eᵢ)P(A|Eᵢ).
- **Bayes:** P(Eᵢ|A) = P(Eᵢ)P(A|Eᵢ)/ΣP(Eⱼ)P(A|Eⱼ).

## 3. Random Variables
- Probability distribution; **mean (expectation)** E(X) = Σxᵢpᵢ; variance = Σxᵢ²pᵢ − [E(X)]².

## 4. Binomial Distribution
- P(X=r) = ⁿCᵣ pʳ q^{n−r}; mean = np, variance = npq.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Mutually exclusive ≠ independent.
- Bayes' theorem for posterior (reverse conditional) probability.
- Binomial mean np, variance npq (variance < mean).

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Conditional & total probability.
- Bayes' theorem.
- Binomial: mean np, variance npq.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
For 'given that... find probability of cause' problems, use Bayes' theorem directly.
